data.title: RWA doesn't lack assets, it lacks buyers.
data.description:
Author: JeanW Source: X, @JeanW0708

When analyzing Hong Kong market cases, a characteristic was found: The product structure was complete, and the compliance path was followed, but there were no genuine external buyers.

For example, RWA STHK, with a total size of HK$17.6 million, after reviewing the announcements, it was found that more than half was purchased by the chairman's own BVI company, and the placement was completed four days before the end of the fiscal year; RWA was used for the company's financial statements. Delin Holdings' property tokenization included a HK$60 million allocation in the announcements from the project's inception, essentially "gifting" its own assets to target users.

This illustrates that the problem is not in the execution, but in the asset selection: The tokenization of some assets will not create any value that traditional finance cannot provide, and the motivation for some is not even related to tokenization itself.

Therefore, it's necessary to establish an asset evaluation framework, even a simple one. Question 1: Is this asset worth tokenizing? If not tokenized, will the demand for this asset be met? **Put the asset on two axes:** Horizontal axis: Access friction in traditional finance. This includes geographical restrictions, legal exclusion, minimum investment amounts, and trading time windows. The greater the friction, the higher the "pipeline value" of tokenization. Vertical axis: Real demand density. How many people want exposure to this asset? Is the demand structural or speculative?

Four Quadrants:

High Demand + High Friction → Where RWA Should Be Exist.Tokenized US Treasury bonds are currently the most successful case. A large number of institutions and individuals worldwide need risk-free return exposure, but different investor groups face different types of friction: non-US investors lack a legal channel to directly purchase T-Bills, on-chain protocols and DAO treasuries lack native risk-free return assets, and even US accredited investors face efficiency friction due to slow settlement and lack of composability in traditional funds.

Ondo's USDY opens up US Treasury yield exposure to non-US users through Reg S, resolving access friction; OUGG, based on Reg D, targets US accredited investors, resolving efficiency friction. Different products, different compliance paths, and different frictions combine to support a scale of tens of billions.

Ondo's USDY opens up US Treasury yield exposure to non-US users through Reg S, resolving access friction; OUGG, based on Reg D, targets US accredited investors, resolving efficiency friction.

Different products, different compliance paths, and different frictions, combined, support a scale of tens of billions.

Pre-IPO > US Stocks > US Treasuries. Tokenized gold benefits from physical storage and liquidity friction, independent of policy windows. Pre-IPO equity benefits from structural friction in the PE/VC exit cycle; as long as the holding period in the primary market doesn't shorten, the gaps won't disappear. Tokenized US stocks benefit from global entry barriers; global regulators have significant disagreements regarding security-type RWAs. Tokenized US Treasuries benefit from interest rate cycles; the on-chain world lacks a window of risk-free returns, and a 200 basis point rate cut eliminates the interest rate spread, rendering the product logic worthless.

data.title: A "double-edged sword" interest rate cut strategy? Will Warsh's move give Trump what he wants?
data.description: When Trump chose Kevin Warsh as the next Federal Reserve Chairman, the market widely believed that Warsh must have somehow convinced the president that he could persuade other Fed policymakers to agree to lower interest rates. But the question is, how? For months, one fact has become very clear: among the Fed's 19 top policymakers, few support rate cuts. Fed Governor Milan, who is closest to the White House, has been the "lone voice" advocating for significant rate cuts. But economists say Warsh may be devising a path to get Fed officials to agree to loosen monetary policy: shrinking the Fed's balance sheet. Warsh's previous speeches indicate that he believes shrinking the $1 trillion balance sheet would have the same impact on the economy as a 50 basis point rate hike. Joe Gagnon, a researcher at the Peterson Institute for International Economics, said Warsh would push his Federal Reserve colleagues to lower short-term interest rates to offset the tightening effect of this balance sheet contraction. Essentially, Warsh would tighten policy with one hand and loosen it with the other. But doing so would indeed bring interest rates down. "Reducing the balance sheet may indeed be an argument Warsh uses to justify 'interest rates should be lower,'" said Mark Cabana, head of U.S. interest rate strategy at Bank of America Global Research. Warsh has been an outspoken critic of the size of the Federal Reserve's balance sheet, which has ballooned from less than $1 trillion before the 2008 financial crisis to more than $6 trillion today. The Federal Reserve currently holds $1.6 trillion in long-term Treasury securities and $1.9 trillion in mortgage-backed securities. This is a huge "footprint" in the financial markets. "If you hold mortgage-backed securities and ultra-long-term Treasury securities, then you are pushing down their yields relative to where these assets should be," Gagnon said. Trump's key ally at the Fed, Milan, recently publicly expressed support for this policy. In a speech calling for the Fed to shrink its balance sheet, Milan stated that the economic impact of balance sheet reduction "can be offset by lowering the federal funds rate." "Therefore, resuming balance sheet reduction will likely require further reductions in the federal funds rate beyond the baseline forecast," he said. Gagnon previously served as a senior official at the Federal Reserve before joining the Peterson Institute. He indicated that in the process of lobbying to become Federal Reserve Chairman, Warsh may have told Trump that he sees a path to lower the federal funds rate by 100 basis points. About 50 basis points of this cut would come in response to the balance sheet contraction, and Warsh might argue that another 50 basis points could be implemented after the higher inflation caused by tariffs and the Iran war subsides. Warsh will face questioning on Tuesday regarding his views on interest rates, when the Senate Banking Committee will hold hearings to consider his nomination as Fed Chairman. Lawmakers will focus on whether Warsh will truly remain independent or simply act according to Trump's wishes. Warsh's spokesperson declined to comment. Warsh will face pressure to cut rates. While significant hurdles remain for Warsh's Senate confirmation, whenever he ultimately takes the helm of the Fed, he will face immense pressure from the White House to lower interest rates. Last week, Trump reiterated his stance that interest rates should be well below the current level of 3.5% to 3.75%. In an interview with Fox Business Network, when asked if he thought interest rates would fall later this year, Trump replied, "I think so when Kevin takes office." He has joked that he would sue Warsh if the Federal Reserve doesn't cut rates significantly. However, many Federal Reserve officials say they are cautious about cutting rates until inflation makes progress toward the 2% target. The war with Iran has pushed inflation to its highest level in two years, making officials even more cautious. Ben Bernanke pioneered the use of the balance sheet during his tenure as Federal Reserve chairman in 2008. At the height of the financial crisis, the Fed lowered interest rates to zero, but this was still insufficient to stimulate the economy. So the Federal Reserve purchased long-term Treasury bonds and mortgage-backed securities to help lower interest rates and boost the economic recovery. In recent years, the Fed has twice attempted to shrink its balance sheet. The first attempt ended in the 2019 market crash, with money market rates soaring. The second attempt began in 2022 and ended relatively quietly last December, but the balance sheet still stands at $6.6 trillion, even higher than its 2017 peak. Gagnon stated that Powell and his colleagues have never figured out a good way to communicate overall monetary policy, including the balance sheet. Fed officials tend to relegate the balance sheet program to a secondary position. They often compare it to "watching the paint dry." "The way they talk about it is as if the balance sheet is one thing and setting interest rates is another; they don't talk about the connection between the two at all," Gagnon said. "What's on the balance sheet does influence policy stance, and they should incorporate it into their overall analysis, but they refuse to do so," he added. Since using a balance sheet in 2008, the Federal Reserve has never sold any assets. Instead, it shrinks its balance sheet by waiting for its holdings to mature and then not reinvesting the proceeds. This strategy has left the Fed with a large amount of mortgage-backed securities. But this strategy of slowly exiting assets upon maturity doesn't work the same way for mortgage-backed securities, because most of them are low-interest loans that homeowners are unlikely to prepay, meaning they may not mature for decades. Warsh's Two Paths to Shrinking the Balance Sheet Bank of America's Cabana stated that the Fed has two options for shrinking its balance sheet. First, it can announce the sale of assets in the open market. He stated that the likelihood of this happening is extremely low. "Selling assets would be incredibly disruptive to financial markets. It would tighten financial conditions and cause a significant decline in the value of risky assets," he said. While an economic slowdown might potentially justify interest rate cuts, the cost would be too high. "I don't think Trump would agree to that at all," Cabana said. In a speech last month, Federal Reserve Governor Milan stated that he would support selling certain securities if they were profitable. But Cabana said he believes other Fed officials would not agree. "If the Fed tries to cash in profits, the market will be very surprised. Most experts will tell you it's highly unlikely. I would also tell you it's highly unlikely," Cabana said. He stated that a more viable option is for the Federal Reserve to focus on the liability side of its balance sheet—the $3 trillion in reserves held by banks at the Fed. Banks hold these reserves in part to meet liquidity standards established after the 2008 financial crisis. Darrell Duffie, a finance professor at Stanford University's Graduate School of Business, stated that reserves are a useful form of liquidity for banks. They are ultra-short-term and highly liquid assets. "This can help banks weather liquidity crises when they desperately need funds," he said on a Brookings Institution podcast. The Fed wants banks to hold ample reserves. At the same time, Duffie noted that the Fed is sensitive to the amount of interest it pays to commercial banks on deposits, as this is often portrayed as "giving away money" to banks. "At least for some people—including some in Congress—it doesn't look very respectable that large banks are getting a lot of interest from the Fed," Duffy said. But he pointed out that there's actually no real cost, as banks can earn the same amount of interest by holding short-term Treasury bills. Cleveland Fed President Hammarck told CNBC last Wednesday that the Fed is struggling to balance the financial stability benefits of ample reserves with some reputational benefits of shrinking its balance sheet. Fed Governor Waller put it more vividly in a February speech: "You don't want banks to be rummaging through their sofa cushions every night looking for money. That's incredibly inefficient and stupid." But there are limits to reducing reserve requirements. "If banks continue to demand reserves, we can only shrink our balance sheet to a certain extent," Hammarck said. Lou Crandall, chief economist at Wrightson ICAP, a research firm specializing in Federal Reserve studies, said central banks are making progress in exploring possible options for reducing banks' excessive reserves. There has been discussion about reforming the liquidity rules implemented after 2008—rules that incentivize banks to hold reserves while establishing programs to ensure banks have access to emergency funding when needed. Duffy stated that another, more controversial idea is for the Federal Reserve to pay lower interest rates on bank reserves exceeding a certain threshold. "If we can find a way to make the banking industry believe it doesn't need to hoard so many reserves, then the Federal Reserve can shrink its balance sheet…it's a win-win for everyone," Crandall said. Bank of America's Cabana stated that if the Federal Reserve can manage to reduce its need for reserves, it will likely stick to its plan to shrink its balance sheet by allowing assets to mature. Guy LeBas, chief fixed-income strategist at Janney Capital Management, said that it took 20 years to develop these complex regulations and policies that require large reserves, and changes to these regulations are "difficult to predict all possible outcomes." "I doubt these policies will change anytime soon," he said. Balance sheet shrinking does not necessarily mean lower interest rates. While Warsh might argue that shrinking the balance sheet should lead to lower interest rates, some experts are skeptical. "While the argument for a 100-basis-point rate cut is broadly plausible, it's far from a done deal," Jon Faust, a former senior advisor to Powell and now a professor of economics at Johns Hopkins University, said in an email. "Creating a story about a rate cut for a Trump eager to do so is a completely different matter from convincing a skeptical FOMC," he added. The FOMC is the body responsible for setting interest rates. Even if it were possible, reforming bank regulation would take a long time. This is unlikely to satisfy an impatient president. "I doubt the federal funds rate will come down before the November midterm elections," Faust said. Bank of America's Cabana stated that he ultimately believes the market will not accept Warsh's argument that a balance sheet reduction achieved in a way that does not disrupt the financial environment justifies interest rate cuts. "That's likely the kind of reasoning they'll use. But the market will be highly skeptical of that argument. If you decide to cut rates, that increases the risk of inflation," he said.

data.title: Why is ERC-8211's new interaction paradigm worth paying attention to?
data.description:
Author: imToken

Starting in 2025, many people may have gradually become accustomed to a new way of interacting: saying to GPT or Gemini, "Please plan my trip to Hong Kong next week and recommend suitable flights and hotels," and it will silently complete a series of steps in the background, such as information searching, condition filtering, route selection, and price comparison, and finally just give you the result for confirmation.

However, bringing the same expectations to the blockchain, the story changes completely.

For example, if you give a DeFi Agent the instruction: "Exchange the ETH in your wallet for USDC, cross over to the Base chain, and then deposit the entire amount into Aave," objectively speaking, from the perspective of "understanding the needs" and "planning the path," today's agents may not necessarily be unable to do this. The real bottleneck lies in the execution phase: You will still likely have to complete operations such as signing, authorizing, exchanging, cross-chain, and depositing step by step, and each step is exposed to the risks of slippage, gas fluctuations, bridging delays, and changes in on-chain state. This also means that if any link deviates from the expected path, the previous actions may not be able to be reversed, and the subsequent actions may not be able to connect. What is left on the chain in the end is often just an unfinished, half-finished process. The problem is not that AI is not smart enough, but that the on-chain execution layer still lacks a way of expression that is truly suitable for agents.

It is precisely for this reason that in early April 2026, Biconomy and the Ethereum Foundation jointly released ERC-8211, which aims to solve the "static limitation" problem in the current execution of smart contracts, and provide a more expressive execution layer for AI agents and complex DeFi workflows, attempting to fill this missing piece of the puzzle.

I. The "Last Barrier" for AI Agents to Connect to the Chain

In the past one to two years, the focus of attention in the crypto industry has clearly shifted from L2 scaling and RWA liquidity to the rather disruptive topic of how AI agents can truly take over on-chain operations.

Objectively speaking, from "issuing multi-step DeFi strategies using natural language" to "allowing autonomous agents to manage an entire cross-chain investment portfolio," we have recently seen numerous practical applications, and most of these concepts are already mature at the demo level. This includes generating multi-step DeFi strategies using natural language, autonomous rebalancing, automatic yield migration, cross-chain position adjustments, and even more complex portfolio management. From a reasoning and orchestration perspective, AI's capabilities are already quite advanced. However, when truly deployed in a production environment, the shortcomings at the execution layer become increasingly apparent. In a production setting, this shortcoming can be summarized in one sentence: DeFi is dynamic, but most batch processing today remains static. The official website and discussion threads for ERC-8211 clearly explain this issue: while existing ERC-4337 and EIP-5792 have indeed advanced the old model of "one signature corresponding to one call" to a new stage of "one signature can package multiple calls," the parameters in these calls are still largely frozen at the moment of signing. In other words, the amount, target value, and expected output entered by the user during signing will not be automatically adjusted due to changes in the on-chain state during actual execution. However, DeFi itself is inherently full of uncertainty. The actual output of a swap depends on the slippage and liquidity of the block being executed; the arrival time and final amount of a bridge transaction depend on the bridge's mechanism and fees; and the share-to-asset ratio of lending protocols or Vaults is constantly changing. After all, the values ​​seen by users or agents when signing are often just a current estimate, not the actual result at the time of execution. To understand what ERC-8211 solves, consider a classic example: suppose an agent wants to do something seemingly ordinary—convert ETH in their account into USDC and then deposit the entire amount into Spark to earn interest. Under the existing static batch processing model, the agent must estimate how much USDC it will receive after the swap before signing. This often forces you to pre-define the input amount for the second step during signing. If the estimate is too high, the actual amount received will be insufficient, causing the entire batch to roll back. If the estimate is too low, some funds will remain idle in the wallet and cannot be used. In other words, it essentially falls into a dilemma: either bear the risk of failure or bear the opportunity cost. This is why many seemingly simple on-chain processes quickly become vulnerable once the steps are extended to 5, 8, or even across two chains. This is not because the strategy itself is too complex to describe, but because the existing execution paradigm relies too heavily on pre-defineable parameters. In short, the upper limit of static batch processing capability actually determines the upper limit of the strategies that the agent can safely execute. From this perspective, what ERC-8211 aims to solve is not how the AI ​​Agent makes decisions, but rather whether there is a more natural, stable, and secure way to execute those decisions on-chain after they have been made. This allows on-chain execution to, for the first time, have an expression form natively designed for the AI ​​Agent. II. What exactly does ERC-8211 change? The core breakthrough of ERC-8211 is not about cramming more steps into a single signature, but about upgrading batch processing from a sequence of transactions with hard-coded parameters to a "program where parameters are dynamically evaluated during execution." This may sound abstract, but it's not difficult to understand. The official description uses the phrase: From transactions to programs. This means that ERC-8211 no longer treats a batch as a list of actions to be executed sequentially, but rather as an executable program that is evaluated at runtime and subject to safety conditions. Specifically, it achieves this through three composable primitives: Fetchers: Define where the parameter is retrieved from. This can be a query for the current balance of an address, making the parameter no longer a snapshot at the time of signing, but a real-time reading fetched from the on-chain state at the moment of execution; Constraints: After the parameter is resolved, it must undergo inline constraint checks—for example, "the received USDC must be at least ≥ 2500," or "slippage cannot exceed..." "0.5%", these constraints are validated before the value is routed into the next call. If any one fails, the entire batch is immediately rolled back; Predicates (triggering conditions): can be understood as gatekeepers between steps. They are not responsible for generating values, but for determining whether to continue execution. For example, in cross-chain scenarios, the Ethereum-side batch can use a predicate to guard the condition "the WETH from the cross-chain has arrived", and will not commit until it arrives; In this design, each parameter must answer two questions: First, where should this value come from when it is executed? Second, what conditions must be met before it is actually used in the call? With these three factors combined, a batch is no longer just a sequence of transactions, but a program with embedded security checks. Ultimately, the mental model of static batch processing is a list—executing steps A, B, and C sequentially; while the mental model of ERC-8211 is a conditional program—after A is executed, the actual output of A is used as the input of B; B only proceeds to C if the constraints are met; if any step fails to meet expectations, the entire batch is rolled back. We can essentially understand it as a "smart batch processing" mechanism specifically designed for AI agents and complex DeFi operations, because in traditional on-chain operations, completing a complex DeFi strategy often requires multiple independent transactions: withdrawing funds from a lending protocol, exchanging tokens, and then depositing them into another protocol (further reading: "A Panoramic View of Crypto AI Protocols: Starting from Ethereum's Main Battlefield, How to Build a New Operating System for AI Agents?"). Each step requires a separate signature and confirmation, which is cumbersome for human users and a bottleneck for AI agents that need high-frequency autonomous operations. ERC-8211's solution allows multiple blockchain operations to be combined and executed in a single transaction. Each step dynamically parses the actual value during execution and must meet predefined conditions before proceeding to the next step. For example, an agent can complete the following in a single signed transaction: withdraw funds from Aave → exchange the received amount on Uniswap → store the exchange result in Compound—all executed atomically without writing new smart contracts. III. Why is it more relevant to wallets, especially smart wallets? ERC-8211 deserves the attention of the wallet industry not only because it is suitable for agents, but also because it will redefine the wallet's position in the interaction chain. In the past, wallets were more like secure signers. Their responsibilities included storing private keys, displaying transactions, obtaining user confirmation, and then sending out the signature. This role was crucial in the EOA era and continues to hold true in the era of account abstraction. However, if more and more on-chain operations are handled by agents, the wallet's role will become increasingly central and crucial. The reason is simple: when users no longer manipulate on-chain actions one by one but instead authorize an agent to execute an entire set of objectives, the wallet must be able to handle this higher-level interaction. It will no longer just display a contract address and a piece of calldata, but an entire execution procedure encompassing "intent—value retrieval logic—conditional judgment—final result." Therefore, future wallets will need to understand not just transactions, but entire programs. ERC-8211 provides a clearer framework for wallets at this layer because it explicitly writes these execution semantics into the coding structure. This includes where the parameters come from, what conditions must be met, when to continue, and when to roll back. These are not black boxes hidden in the backend logic, but objects that can be interpreted, simulated, and displayed by the wallet. From a wallet's perspective, this entire mechanism ultimately points to the same thing: users are no longer signing a string of low-level calls that they can hardly understand, but rather signing an execution program that is result-oriented, clearly defined, and conditionally verifiable. The AI ​​Agent is responsible for understanding the user's intent and generating the path; the wallet is responsible for presenting this path to the user for review in a clearer way; and the relayer is only responsible for submitting when the conditions are met and does not have the authority to tamper with the result. This is precisely why non-custodial execution is considered a prerequisite for Agentic DeFi, because while agents can participate, sovereignty, constraints, and final settlement remain on-chain, which is also ERC-8211. What truly makes it compatible with smart wallets is that it incorporates the concept of "securely expressing complex intentions" into its protocol-level standards. It's worth noting that ERC-8211 is fully compatible with account abstraction frameworks such as ERC-4337, EIP-7702, and ERC-7579. It doesn't replace account abstraction but rather adds a layer of procedural execution semantics to the Agent on top of it. If ERC-4337 solves the problem of "who can initiate transactions on my behalf," and EIP-7702 solves the problem of "how the EOA temporarily possesses smart contract capabilities," then ERC-8211 solves the problem of whether, once the Agent begins operating on my behalf, it can complete an entire decision chain within a single signature. Looking back at the evolution of Ethereum's on-chain interaction paradigm over the past 10 years: Phase 1: One signature = One function call (EOA era) Phase 2: One signature = A set of statically packaged calls (ERC-4337, EIP-5792 era) Phase 3: One signature = A dynamically evaluated intent program (ERC-8211 era) Each leap means that users (or agents representing users) can express more complex goals with less friction. Although ERC-8211 is still in the draft stage, technical discussions are ongoing, and large-scale protocol adoption will take time, the direction it points to is clear enough: when AI agents truly begin making on-chain decisions for humans, the on-chain system will need a matching, native execution syntax.

data.title: Hong Kong Web3 Carnival: A watershed moment for Web3 as it enters the implementation phase
data.description: The message conveyed by the 2026 Hong Kong Web3 Carnival is significantly different from the industry discussions of the past few years. If the market was previously still repeatedly testing whether "Web3 has real value," then this time, judging from the speeches of Paul Chan, Ip Chi-hang, and Fan Man-chung, the focus of the discussion has fundamentally changed: Web3 is no longer a technical proposition that needs to be proven, but is beginning to enter a phase of institutionalization and structured implementation. More precisely, what Hong Kong is trying to build is not a "Web3 industry cluster," but an operating system for the next generation of financial systems. From “Asset Digitization” to “Financial Restructuring” Hong Kong Financial Secretary Paul Chan repeatedly emphasized the significance of “tokenization” in his speech, clearly pointing out that the types of assets currently included in this process have expanded from early crypto assets to currencies and bonds, real estate, and future income rights. The key to this change lies not in the technological form, but in the shift in the underlying logic of the financial structure. In the traditional financial system, the liquidity, divisibility, and participation barriers of assets are often determined by centralized intermediaries; however, under the tokenization framework, these constraints are recoded into on-chain rules, allowing assets to be split, circulated, and cleared and distributed programmatically. This signifies a more fundamental change: finance is no longer about "who owns assets," but rather "how assets flow." In the past, finance involved a minority allocating assets; under the new framework, assets begin to flow to a wider range of participants with less friction. Therefore, the essence of tokenization is not simply "on-chain," but rather a shift in the financial resource allocation mechanism from "institution-driven" to "rule-driven." This shift provides the foundation for larger-scale financial innovation in the future. The Introduction of AI: From Efficiency Tool to Economic Agent If tokenization reshapes "assets," then the introduction of artificial intelligence begins to reshape "participants." The "DAE (Decentralized Agentic Economy)" framework proposed by Fan Wenzhong, a member of the Executive Committee of the China Finance Society and former chairman of Beijing Financial Holdings Group, offers a forward-looking explanation: in the future economic system, AI will no longer be merely a tool to assist decision-making, but rather an economic agent with independent action capabilities. This judgment is based on three key premises: AI agents possess continuously evolving decision-making capabilities, enabling them to make strategy choices in complex environments; blockchain provides them with identity, accounts, and a verifiable execution environment; and programmable currency and smart contracts enable them to directly participate in value exchange. Within this framework, the financial system will undergo a fundamental change: trading behavior will no longer be entirely driven by humans, but will gradually shift to a hybrid model of "machine participation and rule constraints." This not only means improved trading efficiency, but also a restructuring of market operating logic. For example, in areas such as high-frequency trading, liquidity management, and cross-market collaboration, the combination of AI agents and on-chain infrastructure may significantly reduce the original intermediary links. This is why Paul Chan emphasized: "This interest (AI agents) and the intersection of Web3 and artificial intelligence will change the 'rules of the game.'" The key at the institutional level: from "exploration" to "executability" A clear technological path does not automatically mean that the system can operate. The key to its successful implementation lies in the institutional arrangements. In this regard, the regulatory authorities, represented by Ip Chi-hang, Executive Director of the Intermediaries Division of the Hong Kong Securities and Futures Commission, have given a relatively clear signal: Hong Kong is moving the digital asset system from "policy statement" to "implementation framework." Over the past year, Hong Kong has completed three key leaps: Systematic expansion of regulatory scope—exchanges, custody, staking, and derivatives are gradually being incorporated into a unified system, providing a foundation for institutional entry; Gradual liberalization at the product level—from tokenized funds to money market instruments, regulation is forming a replicable product path; Clarification of infrastructure direction—especially stablecoins, which are beginning to enter the core policy focus. The combined result of these three points is that Web3 in Hong Kong has moved from "discussable" to "executable." Stability and Continuity: The Differentiated Advantages of the Hong Kong Path Globally, attitudes towards Web3 vary significantly across different regions. The United States has frequently adjusted its policies, leading to uncertainty in institutional expectations; Europe, on the other hand, primarily adopts strict regulation, which to some extent restricts innovation. In contrast, Hong Kong has chosen a different path: Adhering to the principle of "same risk, same regulation," it gradually releases space for innovation through a sandbox mechanism, while maintaining continuity in policy pace. The "stability and transparency" emphasized by Hong Kong Legislative Council member Chiu Tat-kan in his speech, while seemingly a principled statement, actually constitutes a significant institutional advantage in the current global regulatory environment: When uncertainty becomes the norm, stability itself becomes a scarce resource. This point is particularly crucial for long-term capital and institutional participants. From Industry Competition to System Competition In summary, Hong Kong is not advancing a single technology or industry, but rather a systemic construction: Asset digitization pathways represented by RWA; a settlement network centered on stablecoins; and a restructuring of economic entities driven by AI agents. These three main threads are gradually coupling on the same infrastructure, ultimately pointing not to who is building Web3, but to who can define how the next-generation financial system operates. In this sense, Hong Kong's role is shifting from participant to rule-maker. When assets, rules, and participants change simultaneously, the financial system often enters a phase of accelerated restructuring. The current question may no longer be whether Web3 has long-term value, but rather how the new power structure will be redistributed in this round of systemic restructuring.

data.title: Unable to afford tokens, I've become part of the lower-tier market segment in the AI ​​era.
data.description:
By: Huang Yiting

What will be the most "luxurious" consumption in human work in 2026? The answer is not buying a top-of-the-line computer or acquiring a few impressive pieces of clothing, but rather being able to use the world's most advanced AI tools without restrictions or regard to cost.

This means you won't have to rack your brains to optimize prompts to control costs, fearing a pop-up message saying "Today's free quota has been used up"; nor will you have to compare repeatedly, reluctant to burden your beloved Claude (a large language model developed by the American AI company Anthropic), and only be able to delegate less important tasks to cheaper, lighter models.

AI is certainly useful, but every use comes with a cost, and the consumption of tokens is so high that it's almost unaffordable. Being meticulous and cautious has become the most realistic state of affairs for AI "engineers" today. This is reminiscent of the dial-up internet era twenty years ago. Back then, bandwidth was scarce and expensive, and developers, in order to save website bandwidth, compressed images and simplified code as much as possible, almost never daring to upload videos. Video startups like Tudou.com were extremely rare, and the bandwidth consumption from videos became a major cost of website operation. Yesterday is repeating itself. In the AI ​​industry chain, computing power flows like water from top to bottom. Starting from upstream GPUs (Graphics Processing Units) and data centers, and passing through cloud vendors and model providers, it is encapsulated into APIs (Application Programming Interfaces) and ultimately flows to developers and ordinary users, becoming specific calls and quantifiable tokens. While seemingly intangible, it corresponds to clear costs at every stage: GPU depreciation, power consumption, and high-bandwidth storage all contribute to the final bill. Now, this pipeline is becoming congested. On one hand, demand is exploding; complex inference scenarios such as multimodal and agent-based systems are causing token consumption to increase a thousandfold. On the other hand, supply remains constrained; GPUs, HBM (High-Bandwidth Memory), power, and data center construction all have physical limitations, and GPU utilization remains low. Intelligence comes at a price. While explosive growth has made token prices cheaper, the cost of using them has increased dramatically. The price increases are cascading down the supply chain. Upstream GPUs are scarce and in high demand, leading to a shortage of computing power. Midstream cloud providers have been the first to adjust prices. Amazon Web Services, Google Cloud, Baidu Cloud, and Alibaba Cloud have all raised fees for some AI-related services in the past quarter. Model providers have also ended their subsidy cycles, with Tencent and Alibaba successively ceasing free public testing and increasing API call prices. Tencent's Hunyuan large model saw the highest price increase of 463%. These price increases on the model and application sides mean that computing power is no longer an abstract concept exclusive to giants; it's now being used in the form of tokens, providing a paid service to everyone. Just like data traffic in the past, priced in MB (megabytes of mobile data), account holders could easily run out of credit and have their services suspended. Jensen Huang recently proposed the concept of "Token Economics," arguing that inference has become the core workload of AI, and tokens are the new commodities—standardized, measurable, and tradable. Thus, tokens have evolved from a technological byproduct of model training into a core production factor driving the digital economy. According to Huang, tokens, as commodities, vary in quality. From the free tier to the top tier, the price of one million tokens ranges from $0 to $150. Low-latency, high-interaction tokens (such as real-time dialogue and autonomous driving) require expensive computing power and are priced high; high-throughput, offline-processing tokens (such as large-scale offline inference and batch data processing) are not sensitive to latency, can be produced with cheap computing power, and are priced low. Tokens have already been stratified as "commodities," but what about the people using them? Perhaps in the future, the definition of the "lower-tier market" population will no longer be limited to whether they can afford physical goods. AI Users, Swept Up by Anxiety "Am I not a valued member?" On the evening of March 11th, Su Yu looked at the pop-up window on her computer screen, somewhat annoyed. The pop-up reminded her that her token usage for the week had reached 90% of the limit, and that the use of related models would be suspended after the limit was exhausted until the limit was updated next week. Su Yu is a doctoral student at a university, currently preparing her graduation thesis. For the past three years, Google's Gemini and OpenAI's ChatGPT have been her best partners, and she is a loyal subscriber to these two "AI long-term workers." In mid-February of this year, Claude from Anthropic also joined her team and quickly became one of her most trusted. "Claude is so useful; it's such a powerful tool," Su Yu said. She had several AI applications simultaneously help her organize and design research models. ChatGPT's answers lacked logical rigor, Gemini was too flamboyant and flattering, only Claude, like an objective and professional senior consultant, read through the client's requirements word by word before outputting a truly usable and inspiring solution. After using it for free for more than half a month, Su Yu spent about 180 RMB to subscribe to Claude's monthly membership. Compared to Gemini and ChatGPT, Claude is unique in that it also sets daily and weekly limits on token consumption for its members. This is understandable, as according to LMARaena, a globally renowned blind testing leaderboard for large models, Claude's flagship model, Claude-Opus-4-6-thinking, ranked first globally as of March 20th. However, Su Yu had never experienced such a direct token restriction. The first time she triggered Claude's limit mechanism was on a Wednesday; she was only halfway through understanding "grounded theory" before she could no longer access it, leaving her with a sense of helplessness, a feeling of "academic stagnation." Having become accustomed to Claude's assistance, she found it difficult to return to her initial research mindset. She tried to "manually" consult the most original theoretical books, but the efficiency was extremely low, and she didn't fully trust some translated materials. "In the end, I still had to wait for Claude to resume operation before reviewing everything." The four days of waiting were agonizing. The Claude usage limit was causing Su Yu extreme anxiety. One Tuesday, Su Yu sent a screenshot of her Claude backend, showing that she had already used 45% of her weekly limit. "It's only been two days this week! I've been using it very sparingly, only discussing one paper topic a day, and it's already reached the limit!" Su Yu was somewhat distraught. Who says AI can't replace humans? This AI is almost more difficult to deal with than her supervisor. [Image of Su Yu's Claude backend] Image source: Interviewee

She has developed the habit of checking the backend every time she asks a question, afraid of running out of ideas. Thinking back to how she used to chat with Claude and have him help her create PPTs, she inwardly scolded herself for wasting time.

This cautious use of "good models" is gradually becoming common.An AI film and television industry entrepreneur told me that when his team uses ByteDance's AI video model "Jimeng," they simultaneously access the APIs of several other model vendors. "The better models are indeed more expensive, so we can only switch between different models to balance costs."

Not long ago, Jimeng lowered its membership points limit, which he felt was normal, "The C-end was already subsidized; now it's just taking back a portion."

On the other hand, they worry about their own situation, lamenting, "Now it's even more unaffordable." Rising AI costs can sometimes directly jeopardize the lifeline of small startups. End users are anxious about tokens, and model manufacturers are also anxious about computing power costs. Regarding the reasons for the surge in token usage, Wang Jian, an academician of the Chinese Academy of Engineering, previously used the development of electricity as an analogy: early AI applications were like "lighting up a lamp," consuming limited electricity. However, the new generation of applications, represented by OpenClaw (intelligent agents), are like turning on an "air conditioner," requiring increasingly more electricity. However, Wang Jian emphasized that this growth not only signifies the widespread adoption of applications but also the decrease in the cost of individual tokens. "If electricity prices don't drop, ordinary people won't be able to afford air conditioning." However, compared to the simple question-and-answer calls of the early days, more and more tasks are now completed through agents. Models need to break down problems, call tools, write code, debug, and then correct them. A seemingly simple request often corresponds to multiple rounds of inference and multiple API calls, exponentially increasing token consumption. Although the unit price has decreased, the overall computing power cost is higher. "As the model has become larger, the inference cost has also increased accordingly. We also hope to bring it back to its normal commercial value. Long-term reliance on low-price competition is not conducive to the development of the entire industry, which is also one of our considerations," said Zhang Peng, CEO of Zhipu. In the past two months, Zhipu has raised the price of its GLM (Zhipu's large language model) series models three times, with some model prices approaching the pricing level of leading international models. Zhang Peng's other concern is that "the biggest problem we face in the next 12 months may be computing power. All technologies, including intelligent agent frameworks, have increased the creativity and efficiency of many people by 10 times. But the prerequisite is that everyone can use them. We can't let insufficient computing power cause an agent to think for a long time without giving me an answer." According to Claude's calculation method, 100 Tokens are roughly equivalent to 75 English words or 50 Chinese characters, and the price of the output of a Token is five times the price of the input—this is a very simple conversion. In other words, every answer from AI must be carefully considered. The tokens consumed by the background thinking, querying, generation, and even errors caused by model illusions will all be calculated and ultimately become a real-money bill. Lin Zhijia, founder of AGI (AI Encoding Intelligence), did the math. He raised four "lobsters" (AI tokens), some deployed locally and some in the cloud. Taking the cloud deployment as an example, he purchased a monthly Coding Plan (AI coding subscription service) for about 30-40 RMB. With nine days left in March, his token consumption was less than 10% of the plan's limit—as a media professional, his need for tokens was actually not significant. However, billing by tokens wasn't cost-effective. “If I just have it send me a news message at 9 AM every day, the tokens it consumes cost about 0.9 RMB, which is about 20 RMB over 30 days, almost the same as the cost of a Coding Plan. Sometimes there are also losses and model updates, which can consume three or four RMB worth of tokens just for updates.” Weighing the pros and cons between different billing methods has become a daily routine for frequent users, and every penny spent on purchasing tokens ultimately points to the same thing—computing power, and the corresponding GPU depreciation costs and data center power consumption. GPUs have become the starting point for everything; the supply of high-end chips determines the upper limit of the entire system. “Except for the backup machines reserved for some customers, we've basically sold out; not a single card is left,” said Liu Hua, Deputy General Manager of UCloud's Architecture Technology Center. Below the GPUs, data centers, networks, and storage systems must also be built—high-speed interconnection and low-latency transmission are not "plug-and-play" standard components. Liu Hua mentioned that the cost of network and storage alone can account for about 20% of the total computing power cost. Next, there are model vendors and API service providers. They deploy large models on this infrastructure, encapsulating them into standardized interfaces for developers to use. In the past two years, these roles have begun to overlap, with cloud vendors selling computing power and providing model APIs, gradually becoming the central hub connecting GPUs, models, and developers.

● A diagram illustrating how computing power flows. Image source: AI generated

Computing power thus penetrates downwards layer by layer, with the latest change occurring on the demand side of industries. “Previously, AI was mostly paid for by B2B companies, but now it’s becoming increasingly common for B2C companies to pay,” said Lin Zhijia. Models are encapsulated into APIs, the entry point is simplified, and the barrier to entry is lowered, allowing individual developers and even ordinary users to directly access the underlying computing power. “Now, basically everyone just needs to browse social media to know how to use it.” Computing power is even showing a trend towards retailization. Around 2024, some cloud vendors began launching GPU “daily cards,” lightweight cloud servers, and even “one-click deployment” trial products. For example, UCloud’s 6.9 yuan trial package for “shrimp farmers” is essentially more like a ticket, packaging complex environment configuration and computing power scheduling, allowing users to try it out at a very low cost. “Many people are actually here to ‘test the waters’ or try something new,” Liu Hua said. “Everyone is a little anxious, afraid of falling behind.” But lower barriers to entry do not mean lower costs. In Liu Hua’s view, “Using the analogy of the development stages of the Internet, the current cost of computing power is undoubtedly still in an early stage of development, a very expensive stage.” This is why developers are careful with their spending, and platforms are hesitant to easily expand the scale of resource usage. Even leading companies are making trade-offs. OpenAI's previous shutdown of its video generation project, Sora, was interpreted by many industry insiders as a trade-off between computing power and return on investment. With limited resources, they prioritized focusing on more core model capabilities and business operations. The recent adjustments made to AI businesses by internet giants like Alibaba, Tencent, and ByteDance also reflect a focus on concentrating computing resources. Everyone is realizing one thing: the future competition will not be about the scale of computing power, but about the efficiency of computing power utilization. The chain reaction caused by the shortage of computing power is like a long rainy season in the AI ​​era, and everyone living in this era will inevitably experience its challenges. What happens when computing power flows to the end? Su Yu is trying to allocate and schedule computing resources. She has divided different models into layers: ChatGPT is used for writing official documents and compiling briefings, Gemini is responsible for drawing diagrams and handling language details, and Claude is used specifically for the most critical aspects, such as research frameworks, designing ideas, and analyzing long texts. This ensures that both her efficiency and budget are maximized. For example, she recently processed a batch of interview materials, first having Claude provide an analysis framework, and then "throwing" this framework to Gemini for initial coding. “I trust Claude’s guidance more, but I can delegate the detailed work to cheaper models.” If Claude didn’t have a limit, she would even stop using Gemini. Of course, this isn’t an advertisement for Claude; it’s just that Su Yu believes this application is more suitable for her needs. Good models are becoming scarce, and scarce resources are only used where they are most critical. To further save money, many users, like Su Yu, have started cutting costs on the details. On social media, it became popular to use classical Chinese to converse with AI, because shorter characters meant fewer tokens. Some argue that blindly following the trend of saying “hello” and “thank you” to AI is also an unnecessary waste of resources. After all, AI doesn’t need emotional value. In fact, much waste is not within the user's control; sometimes it's a problem with the model's access and operation. Not long ago, Luo Fuli, head of the MiMo large model team, mentioned, "I can't strictly calculate the losses caused by third-party harness integration, but I've seen OpenClaw's context management up close, and it's terrible. In a single user query, it triggers multiple rounds of low-value tool calls, each round issued as an independent API request, and each request often carries a context window exceeding 100K tokens. The actual number of requests is several times that of the native Claude Code framework. Converted to API pricing, the real cost is probably dozens of times the subscription price." Returning to the issue of usage, users actively conserve tokens, and platforms dare not fully unleash their user base. This cost-saving "constraint" presents a contradiction, as OpenAI is currently facing—it generated $4.3 billion in revenue in the first half of 2025, while during the same period, the company suffered a net loss of $13.5 billion, meaning that for every dollar OpenAI earns, it loses three dollars. The bulk of the losses comes from investments in computing power. Currently, computing power is no longer just a matter of availability, but rather the sustainability and extent of its use. When AI becomes sufficiently useful, people will reorganize their work methods around it; when tokens become expensive and limited, this new organizational structure itself will be forced to shrink. If computing power cannot truly become as ubiquitous as electricity in the future, AI will inevitably lead to differentiation, further widening the cognitive gap between people. For example, Su Yu doesn't intend to fully share her AI usage methods with those around her. How to interact with Professor Claude and what kind of data to feed him are her little secrets, and in the short term, they are her competitive advantage. If a colleague asks her to recommend a good model, she strongly recommends Gemini and ChatGPT, "Of course, DeepSeek is also a good choice." Su Yu winked mischievously. In an era where "one-person companies (OPCs)" and "super-individuals" are gaining popularity, such subtle tactics are not uncommon. When the usability of AI is correlated with quantifiable tokens, the real difference lies in how people use it. (Su Yu is a pseudonym in this article) Cover Source: "Cosmic Exploration Editorial Department" References: Emerging Intelligence: "Yang Zhilin/Zhang Peng/Xia Lixue/Luo Fuli/Huang Chao, Talking about Lobsters and 'Token Economics'" Daily Economic News: "AI Drives Massive Token Consumption and Memory Hardware Shortage" Amid the computing power leasing boom, operators are ramping up their liquid-cooled server deployments. (Related articles: "Zhipu's Zhang Peng: When the model is strong enough, the API itself is the best business model"; "Zhipu's stock price hits a record high, and the price of its new generation model increases by another 10%"; "Deep Tide TechFlow: Tokens go global, selling Chinese electricity to the world"; "Silicon Star Pro: Luo Fuli: Wake up, everyone, it's time to end the token frenzy").

data.title: Interview with He Yi: I don't recommend selling your house to go all in on crypto; survival is the priority in the crypto world.
data.description:
Note: This article is a dialogue between Binance Co-CEO He Yi and Du Yu, head of Wanxiang Blockchain Labs, at the Web3 Carnival in Hong Kong on April 26.

Du Yu: Hello everyone! I am Du Yu from Wanxiang Blockchain Labs. I feel your enthusiasm today. No need for introductions, please say hello to everyone.

He Yi: First of all, thank you very much for coming to Hong Kong to see me. Actually, I'm here to see you all too. I'm very happy to see you all here. Hello everyone! I am Xiao He, Binance's "Chief Customer Service Officer." Thank you!

Du Yu: It's an honor to have you here at the event. We also had a dialogue on this stage last year, when you were online. It's a great honor to have you here today.

Du Yu: It's an honor to have you here today. This past year has seen many interesting developments in the industry, especially the impact and opportunities brought by AI. I feel it's a pivotal moment of diverse convergence, and having this dialogue with you here is particularly interesting. In this complex cycle, I saw many people asking you specific questions at yesterday's AMA. Today, I'd like to talk more about your perspective on the industry. Because there are people from various fields here—traditional finance, startups—and I heard many students flew in specifically to hear your speech. In the market environment of the past six months, what are your personal interests? What new changes in your schedule, or what you think about most, excite you or even feel a sense of urgency about? He Yi: Regarding AI, as mentioned earlier, my personal life and time allocation, particularly my work, can be roughly divided into three modules: First, the approaching singularity of AI is indeed a moment that no one can ignore; it truly is a moment in human history. Therefore, I spend a lot of time thinking about the potential global transformation brought about by AI and the blockchain industry, and by AI and finance, and what the future of finance will look like. This means I need to think about what Binance's products and services will likely look like ten years from now. Second, I think I spend a lot of time thinking about organization and talent. Often we say that in the early stages of a company, growth may be driven by the founder, co-founder, or individual strengths. However, when a company reaches a certain size, it becomes about organizational effectiveness—how to enable the organization to grow independently and autonomously. This is something I've been constantly thinking about and working on. Third, people often talk about the further integration between cryptocurrencies and traditional finance. Because of this special moment, we've seen both the US and Hong Kong governments showing tremendous support and encouragement for the entire blockchain industry. Therefore, I think we might see a revolutionary change in the industry in the future. Often, when the singularity arrives, when the moment arrives, we are often unaware. It might happen on an ordinary day, but the changes it brings to our lives could mean that all global foreign exchange transactions will take place on the blockchain, no longer the old SWIFT system. We might see all asset transactions no longer limited to a few hours, but could be conducted globally 24/7, like in the cryptocurrency world. Therefore, we will see the whole world rapidly entering a state of "push-back" momentum. Du Yu: You just described a very exciting future. Often when discussing the future, it seems like we're talking about things three, five, or ten years from now, but this year feels different. I think an exponential inflection point is right there, possibly happening today or tomorrow. I think many of you here probably feel this sense of urgency. Following what you just said about AI and the future of finance, let's dive in and discuss some interesting things. Let's start with AI again. Organization is very important in AI, especially for a company like Binance that's almost ten years old. You've probably seen many tech companies laying off employees recently, but their stock prices haven't dropped. It's not because of poor performance, but because of organizational improvements. There's even been a lot of talk about one-person companies lately, but I personally think that's a bit of an exaggeration or we haven't reached that point yet. However, it's certain that companies with three to five or even ten people can do what companies with dozens or hundreds of people used to do. This is already happening. AI is rewriting everything. How has AI changed your personal thinking and decision-making, the entire Binance platform, and even multinational operations? I think Binance faces more challenges than many other large companies because you are a very global company, and you don't have many customers who are more global or distributed than you. What opportunities and challenges has AI brought you? He Yi: First of all, Binance does not plan to conduct large-scale layoffs or cost-cutting and efficiency-enhancing operations like most tech companies. For us, AI is actually about making strong people even stronger. For example, in the past, many people might have thought that a programmer might have innovative and structured thinking when writing code, but their coding skills might be limited. However, today, when AI-powered coding is already very smooth and mature, it can actually improve efficiency by 10 times. I've discovered something quite interesting: we once discovered that an employee was working multiple jobs, and it was because of AI-powered coding that their efficiency improved significantly. At this point, he would feel that the work was easy to handle, so he would take on another job. Of course, he was eventually fired. The overall thinking is to further improve the organization's productivity and innovation capabilities, rather than simply pursuing cost reduction and efficiency improvement at the existing scale. This year, I proposed an idea that others might think is a bit crazy. In the past, we said we wanted to pursue 1 billion users, but this year I told everyone that we should aim for 3 billion users, because we already have 300 million users. What does becoming a company with 3 billion users mean? It means that Binance is not just an exchange; Binance is a global financial infrastructure. It means that our services should be geared towards ordinary people, towards the masses, towards everyone. These services could be daily payments, daily financial management, or every detail of their daily lives. This will push me to encourage employees to be more innovative and try new things. As their business expands its boundaries, their infrastructure needs to be sufficiently stable. I've received many complaints from friends that our product could be made smoother, so I've spent a lot of time optimizing it this year. Give me a few more months, and I'll make it even better. Secondly, regarding the impact of AI on us, I think the biggest impact is that in the past, when we understood AI, we thought of it as a very ambitious child. But if you have a young child at home, you'll find that children sometimes talk nonsense. I don't know if you've seen the movie "A Little Red Flower," directed by Zhang Yuan, where kindergarten children say their teacher is a monster. You'll find that AI and young, highly intelligent children have very similarities. They may not have structured logic; their memories may be discontinuous. In this logic, we've seen a huge leap. The AI ​​of the previous cycle (2025) was actually a highly intelligent brain, but in just a few months today, I feel that AI has already grown its own hands. For example, the lobster that was popular a while ago, and now these Skills, most of the top-tier models have already been equipped with it. This means that AI is no longer just a concept, but has truly begun to enter everyone's lives. Of course, the most obvious example is probably writing code. Why writing code? Essentially because programmers are currently among the highest-paid people in the world. When people talk about how to use AI, the first step is to replace the highest-paid group of people. So I think that in the longer term, today's work is a bit like the workers in the candle factory of the past, but electricity has arrived, and electric lights have arrived. The huge impact and reflection it may bring us is not just about the old paradigm, but about what kind of responsibility you, as an individual or as a company, bear in the whole society and the whole organization when new technological changes arrive. But I don't think this is unusual for individual companies. If you look back at the era of internet celebrities, personal IPs, such as the previous livestreamer Li Jiaqi, can achieve a scale of hundreds of millions, much larger than many companies. But I think the greater reflection is on what part of the responsibility you bear for the public in the whole society and the whole organization. Du Yu: You mentioned earlier that you're aiming to grow from 300 million users to 3 billion users. Actually, when I was preparing for the questions, including when you spoke, my opinion was that you were too conservative. Why? With the development of AI, we ourselves feel that Crypto itself isn't for humans, but for AI. Many people say that in the future, whether you're wealthy will depend on how many robots you have, how much AI you have, and how much computing power you can mobilize. So, have you considered that in Binance's product design, and not just Binance, but the entire Web3 industry, the product design of the entrepreneurs here is geared towards AI users and AI agents? At that point, the user base won't be 300 million or 3 billion, but potentially tens or hundreds of billions of users. The architecture will be different then, and the opportunities it brings to Web3 will be different. Standing here today, it feels like all the traffic and capital have been attracted to AI, but what still excites me is the combination of AI with Web3, and then with robots and robot dogs. This is truly the next generation of the incredibly exciting internet. What are your thoughts on this trend? He Yi: I think AI has entered two different branches, of course there are conflicts and discussions. For example, the path from large language models to Web Coding is one, while physical AI has actually made many breakthroughs. If we must look to the future, you shouldn't see Crypto as just Crypto, but as a more efficient and lower-cost financial system. On such a highly optimized structure, how can AI serve the public more efficiently? A while ago there was a discussion on Twitter where people said that in the West, Anthropic and OpenAI made short videos satirizing each other, while China's AI war was just about delivering milk tea. This question made me think for a long time. I think we shouldn't be technologically arrogant. No matter how amazing or advanced AI technology is, for individuals, for human beings, the most important thing is how this technology serves me and helps improve my life. Whether it's Doubao or Qianwen, when it can tell an elderly woman in rural China that a third-party recommended health product is fake and she shouldn't buy it, I think that's the value and meaning that technology brings to humanity. When a lonely elderly person in a rural area can have someone to chat with every day through Doubao, I think that's the meaning that technology brings to humanity. No matter where humanity goes, we must still remember that we are human, and we must still cherish the green grass and trees.

Du Yu: Especially for people like us with a technical background, sometimes there's an instinct to show off. Many Web3 startups and companies might think it's something very cool, a very cool product, a very cool technology, but in the end, they find there are no users, or they might only be serving a very narrow user base. You're always called "customer service" or "first customer service," but you're also the first product manager. It's very important to understand human nature. All technological advancements, including advancements in financial tools, are for serving better humanity. We are not ultimately serving robots; robots also serve people, and AI is also used to serve people.

... Earlier you mentioned financial infrastructure, and I also believe that the core of Web3 is financial infrastructure, or the next generation of financial infrastructure. A while ago I saw you retweet Binance's Twitter post, which featured two pie charts: Crypto on the left, traditional Trade-Fi on the right, and Binance at the intersection. I think when I first encountered Binance in 2017, I thought the name was really good. I don't know how you came up with that name back then; you wrote about it in your book "My Binance Life," but it seemed to be written rather simply. As I understand it, Finance is easy to understand—it represents existing finance—but B could be Bitcoin, or the entire digital world. In this overlapping or intersecting process, why do you think Binance is iterating and overlapping? What do you think Binance wants to express in the future financial system? He Yi: Back in 2014, the slogan was "Down with Wall Street." Today, as your industry continues to expand, with more and more institutional users and traditional finance embracing crypto, you'll find that this is actually a process of integration. In this integration process, some countries and some regulators are ahead, saying, "I understand what it is, I understand its significance to the financial system." Today, it might be about letting some people try and innovate first. In Hong Kong, companies like Futu and Tiger have already started simultaneously holding equity assets and crypto assets. In the US, companies like Robinhood also have a lot of crypto assets and traditional equity assets. Binance may not just want to tie itself to crypto or so-called equity assets. We recently launched some commodities, including crude oil, gold, and silver. Our platform's commodity trading volume already accounts for a very high proportion of the traditional market. They might now see us as a threat, and they might also shout "Down with Crypto!" But in the longer term, when you want to become part of the financial infrastructure, you don't need to worry too much about whether you're a Crypto or a Trade File, because when we label ourselves, that labeling is a process of self-imposed limitations. When you break through those boundaries and return to the starting point, it's about what you've done for financial freedom in the world, what you've done for financial equality, and whether you've made life better for more ordinary people—that's a very fundamental starting point. Du Yu: You mentioned earlier that the fundamental starting point is very good and important, but sometimes it can be idealistic. Because when we actually do things, there are always compromises with reality and regulation. My question can be approached from two perspectives. First, many traditional mainstream financial institutions are embracing Crypto today, including the two stablecoin licenses issued in Hong Kong last week, which are held by mainstream financial institutions and banks. But sometimes we have doubts. Mainstream financial institutions are already doing well enough; for them, it's just a small innovation that can succeed or fail. Even making stablecoins might negatively impact their revenue because they have to invest, and investment doesn't guarantee success.

From another perspective, from the outsider's viewpoint, that of a crypto native outside the crypto circle. Initially, when people talked about the ups and downs of Bitcoin and cryptocurrencies, I joined Wanxiang Blockchain in 2015. For over a decade, I always felt embarrassed to tell my friends I worked in blockchain every three or four years because the industry was often stigmatized. Even if I said "blockchain" and we were doing technical work, people would think I was just speculating on cryptocurrencies, and they'd label me with a lot of tags. Things have improved a lot in recent years; talking about Bitcoin now brings with it negative associations.

But with the development over the years, crypto native companies are also facing regulation and are becoming more mainstream. From your perspective, which path does Binance prefer, or is it difficult for Binance, which originated from the crypto native world, to follow? How do you think it's more practical to achieve the vision of first principles step by step?

He Yi: The path we're on today is the same one the internet has already traversed. When the internet first emerged in Silicon Valley, there was a strong "hippie culture." Back then, many people thought those involved in the internet and technology were geeks, weirdos. But in that era, the internet branched out dramatically. Those who went left created the dark web, enjoying absolute freedom; those who went right created Google and Amazon, becoming ordinary businesses serving a wider global audience. If you ask me where Binance is headed, we will definitely remain steadfast in our commitment to becoming a business serving the masses. But at the same time, while serving the masses, technological innovation is still extremely important, whether it's blockchain technology or AI technology. Often, people think the stigma and public opinion surrounding cryptocurrencies are normal because if everyone could understand new things, and there were no controversies, how could there be so much hardship, challenges, and continuous innovation? Looking back at my childhood when I went to internet cafes, my mother probably wanted to break my legs. When we see blockchain being embraced by Wall Street and traditional finance in a complete 180-degree turn, it precisely indicates that the industry is becoming increasingly mature, and the early-stage dividends are diminishing. When early dividends decrease, complaints tend to increase. For example, people wonder why Bitcoin isn't falling further, making it impossible to buy at the bottom. Why isn't Bitcoin constantly rising? That's because the early-stage dividend period is over. Once that period is over, the industry enters its so-called maturity stage, and crossing that chasm means ensuring all products have real value and practical applications. I have great respect for many professional technical experts in the industry, but I often ask them a question: What is your technology used for? What problems can it solve? Will anyone be willing to pay for it? I remember discussing this topic in a similar setting last year, and the essence of business remains consistent. However, I believe that technological innovation and continuous iteration are endless. Du Yu: Over the past two years, the focus in the industry has shifted away from technology itself; in fact, technology has become a bottleneck to some extent. Current technology is sufficient for most businesses. What we need to focus on is researching existing technologies. Web3 has many Layer 1 and Layer 2 technologies; the key is how to utilize them, how to change people's lives, and how to gradually build applications. You also mentioned that this industry is gradually entering the mainstream. This year's Hong Kong Web3 Festival, and what we've been doing for the past few years, is attracting more mainstream industries to use Web3 and crypto as valuable tools to help them advance further, just as the internet transformed almost every industry. I believe that Web3, as a new financial infrastructure, will also significantly improve all existing business models. Finally, I have a little time left, and I'd like to talk about some things mentioned in CZ's recent book, *Binance Life*. This book allows more people to understand his journey, especially from his entry into the industry to the present. His growth story is the growth story of Binance, and even more so, the growth story of the entire industry. He wrote many stories in the book, and today we'd like to hear your perspective. How would you summarize it from another perspective as a firsthand witness, or if we push the timeline forward ten or twenty years, how would you recount the events of those ten-plus years? A very important entrepreneurial journey. He Yi: If we go back to 2017, if you look at my WeChat Moments, you'll find that from 2017 until now, I've been posting the phrase, "We're not just starting a business, we're creating history." So, whether it's the past, present, or future, what we're truly doing is figuring out how to use technology to change the future, how to create history. In the course of history, every path you take is naturally not a straight line, not a smooth one. Whether it's the so-called stigmatization of the cryptocurrency world, calling people "you bunch of stinky crypto speculators," or the public's recognition of technological innovation, these are all part of the journey you must take responsibility for. You can't simply understand life as a smooth, uninterrupted journey. If you choose to be a history maker, you also need to bear historical responsibility. This is why, ten years from now, when I describe Binance, I will still say, "We are not starting a business, we are making history." Our slogan has never changed; it has always been "Exchange The World," not just the world's largest cryptocurrency exchange. When you look at it from a longer historical perspective, you'll find that our current discussions about AI's supposed drain on cryptocurrencies, or the impact of AI on cryptocurrencies, are actually normal. The impact of AI isn't just on Web3, or just on cryptocurrencies; it's ubiquitous and inevitable. However, the impact and transformation of Web3 on the entire financial sector is real. Many people will say that all this Web3 stuff is still just trading and finance, and isn't finance a huge industry? If it can improve the efficiency of all finance even a little bit, that's already a remarkable innovation. There's no need to pursue instant success; just take it one step at a time and do a little bit well every day. Du Yu: The initial intention is very important. Some people go astray when making history. You'll find that very few people in this industry can truly persevere for more than ten years. In the book *Binance Life*, I saw many frequently used words; a media outlet even listed them. "Customer," "integrity," etc., truly reflect a heartfelt desire to do one thing well, and this permeates the entire book. Only when your initial intention is correct, and you persist in doing the right thing—perhaps difficult, but the right thing—will you achieve great success after five, ten, or even longer years. He Yi: When doing something difficult but right, people often say, "You make it sound so good, but you're not saints." We're certainly not saints, but for everyone to achieve a win-win situation, what you need to do is, as your platform grows and you have more users, the industry expands, and everyone benefits. Being at the top of the industry naturally makes you a company with greater commercial and public value. I don't believe in purely zero-sum games. I believe that when what you do has value for society, and people pay for it, people are willing to support you, then earning your due is enough. This process is definitely a spiral upward process, accompanied by controversy and pain, but only a small group of people, after achieving financial freedom, will say, "I want to see how far I can go." Du Yu: Many of you here today are already Web3 practitioners, and there's an interesting phenomenon: many of our attendees come from diverse backgrounds. Many have heard of Web3 but haven't actually worked in the industry. We see a lot of people from mainstream, traditional industries. If you were to share a truly insightful observation that only those who have experienced it firsthand could understand, with these guests who are just starting out, considering entering the field, or are simply interested in Web3, what would you say? What advice would you offer? He Yi: Let's get down to business. Many people enter the crypto world because someone tells them a coin has increased tenfold and they should buy it. Traditional Buffett's value investing theory is quite applicable to any industry. If you don't understand an industry, buy top-tier assets. You don't need many choices; just select a few top-tier assets as a starting point to understand the industry.
